speaker: interviewer
text: It is April 21, 1996. We are conducting an interview with Leib, L-E-I-B, Dimerman, D-I-M-E-R-M-A-N, in Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You had mentioned that you improved your status in the Kolmya prison from hard labor to being a librarian and entertainer, and so forth. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Could you talk more about the new role that you took on, what kind of entertainment you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was performing magic-- magician-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] over there in the camp. And I organized some singers. And some people was capable to play accordion. And some people was capable of acrobatic tricks, to do it. And poetry, whatever, any skills you could do-- pick up. And some people was talented, capable young people, older people-- capable. And, even, there was performers, drama performers. And there was one opera actor, too, with us. And I have enough Jews to create something, to do it. And we have very nice, good performance. And then they sent us already, the group, to perform around the other camps. Why, I would say around 350,000, 400,000 people were there--

speaker: interviewer
text: In these other camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: --around Kolmya-- around Kolmya over there. And it was lots of camps. And we used to go in the other camps, which-- no pay. And we used to perform over there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were still prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A matter of fact, even-- they let you free over there to to go, there's nowhere to go. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pretty desolate.

speaker: interviewee
text: --just to die-- the tundra over there and tigers and-- just to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no point in trying to escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely no point even to try.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we are now in the early 1950s?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was in 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how much longer did you continue--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- altogether, it was eight years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how much longer was your prison sentence?

speaker: interviewer
text: 25 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And five years without right to live. I mean, I can't-- after prison, 25 years, I don't supposed to live 105 kilometers away from the big city. And in 10 years, no right to vote.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did this prison term finally come to an end for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The prison came to an end after eight-year term. And I was a librarian. And my obligation was to talk and to re-educated-- to re-educated the people, to tell them, to prove to them they was guilty and they have to correct themselves. It was this kind-- type of work, like social work, to do it. And it was editor for-- in our local, small, little paper. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one time, it came-- from from Moscow, they came, the big shots over there, to check out and have a big meeting. And they have a big job to do it. And I came out with a speech over there. And I told them we're a little bit guilty for the government. We have to say like this. And we're trying the best to correct our mistake, what we did it. In the future, not to call us enemies, to call us friends-- and we're going to prove it, and et cetera. It was a speech. And they was interesting to talk to me after. They called me up. And I told them, usually, you're putting in jail people to correct themselves. He said yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now look, I'm teaching already people the way to correct themselves. Why are you keeping me? I already corrected. I'm teaching people. And after this, they let me free. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And two months later-- then when they let me free, there's nowhere to go, no--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get back to another-- like, where did you go after your left the prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was learning a little bit of my own amateur to paint it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I left to Kavkaz. Kavkaz is over there, touristic places. I used to make pictures in paper, in alabaster. And I used to sell them in the bazaar. And I-- the police used to run after me. I didn't have no license. They're not giving a license over there. And I used to, nighttime, to make different kind of pictures. In Canada, these pictures, they wouldn't even give a penny. Over there, everything is good.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Kavkaz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I used to make-- they used to hang them up on the wall, different kind-- swans and different kind tigers and different-- and I used to sell them. And I make a little bit money. I dress up myself. I was ashamed to come to places where people know me not dressed. And then I would dress up myself. I came over there. I met people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Rowne. And I met some friends. And they told me, Leon, it's time to be married already.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why, you see, to perform, I didn't go anymore after this. My morale, morale was low. And I figure up maybe they'll not let me in the big cities. And I have a stamp. I'm stamped already.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're-- you were labeled as a--

speaker: interviewee
text: As a bad label, you know. When you're in jail in Russia, bad label in society. And they're not accepting you. And I met a cousin, my wife's. And he told me, I want you to be in my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Rowne?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he give me her address. And I came. And she was living with his sister. And just they came from Siberia, and I saw it. My-- I, I didn't overprice myself. I didn't overestimate myself. I know my nerves is not the same like used to be. And I see this is the right person to understand me. And I decided to marry my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. So that's when you met--

speaker: interviewee
text: We fell in love and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --sent letters. And she sent me a couple of letters. And we married. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, excuse me, I missed something. When I-- before this, when I left, I came to Rowne-- after the jail. And I decided I want to go to, to Israel. Then I came to committee to give my papers--

speaker: interviewer
text: To apply.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to apply to let me to go to Israel. I find out where the-- my mother and my sisters and my brothers is in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I find out-- some people received letters from Israel. And they received letters-- a Mrs. Dimerman with the kids is in Israel. And this is the way through somebody I find out. And then they said, no, this is only for Polish people and for Jewish. And you're French. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I'd gone back to my city, to Kostopol. I took witnesses, which I have-- this is what I have a paper right now. And they asked me to publish in newspaper I'm changing my name. And I published in paper I'm changing my name from this to this. It was a court with witnesses. I tell them the reason was admission already. They have admission for the whole criminal from the army. They let them free. They couldn't do me nothing. I tell them what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you wanted to resume your original identity.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they give me my real name.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this, that you went back to your original--

speaker: interviewee
text: Identity-- after I left the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's right after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When I met my wife, I already introduced myself like [LAUGHS] Jewish and Dimerman.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while you were in prison, you were under your Jewish identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was French.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, your French identity--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --while you were in prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Went straight from the army-- after the army, a short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so once again, when did you resume your original--

speaker: interviewee
text: After I came out from prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: You resumed your original identity.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A little bit-- make-- you see, very hard for me to remember. I think-- I think it's after I came out from prison. Just a moment. Yeah, the identity, yes, after I came out from prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1955, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: '56? Mm-hmm. So you [SIGHS] resumed your original identity, and you were in Rowne. And you met your wife, your present wife, Liuba.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, I met my wife in Vilnius, not in Rowne. She was in Vilnius. And I met her over there, which I married my wife. And they didn't let me-- the police didn't let me to live over there in a big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I married Liuba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't they let you live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why, I told you, I have on my passport--

speaker: interviewer
text: That you were a prisoner.

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- yes, a special--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, a stamp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not supposed to live so, so far away from a big city. Then I pay a few underneath the table, and-- 30 kilometers away from the city where Liuba lived. And they give me a stamp. I have the right. Over there, you're supposed to have a stamp in police. When you came in a new city to live, you have to go in police. You have to prove that you have a-- excuse me-- a, a place where to live in so many square meters. And then they give you a permit. You can live over there. They give me a permit over there, 30 kilometers away--

speaker: interviewer
text: From--

speaker: interviewee
text: --which I have problem, too. They said you have to stay 105 kilometers away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of the stamp--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you couldn't be too far from a major city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for a living when you first married?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I married my wife, I was a foreman. And, you know, foundations-- they used to make the foundations. And I learned a little bit the blueprint, slightly. And I took this job, a foreman. When they're making the foundations, they're cutting all the foundations, I was this kind of job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So construction work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was very, very low paid, not high wages.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you living at that time? What was the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was living in Troki, a little city, Troki, T-R-O-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in Lithuania?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- was-- used to be-- yeah, Lithuania. This is from Vilnius, 30 kilometers away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Troki?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think it was around a year. A year, and then we--

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit maybe over a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we left. Son was born--

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: --my older son.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in 1958. In case I [LAUGHS] don't make mistake-- very hard time, and I can't remember. Liuba?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your son was born--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your oldest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and you stayed in Troki for about a year. And where did you go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that, we make the papers already to go to Poland. And we left to Poland-- my wife, me, my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you arrived in Poland around 1959?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where, where in Poland did you settle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wroclaw--

speaker: interviewee
text: Legonice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Legonice. Legonice, L-E-G-N-I-C-A. Legonice.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you pick on Legonice? Why Legonice?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, it's not what I took on Legonice. I was gone where they sent me. When we arrived in Poland, there was already a committee which they connected with the ORT organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: ORT.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they sent us. And the UNRRA was over there, too. And they sent us to Legonice. In Legonice, they provide us with a place where to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that? The ORT organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- and a house. And they give me-- they give me so much money monthly to live and to learn a trade, which I can use for Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What trade were you being trained in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which date-- I have a little bit experience from the army in welding. And, like, a welder, I was learning over there. I was in school, and I was learning welding. And the ORT paid us support.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the ORT organization was training you, as well as giving you an allowance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And this give me opportunity to learn to be a welder, which I'm not the only one. Lots of people was attending the school from the ORT organization. And then we put application to go to Israel, which I sent my wife first--

speaker: interviewer
text: When--

speaker: interviewee
text: --with my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did she leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: She left before me.

speaker: interviewee
text: '60, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do after she left for Israel? How did you spend your time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in-- I was over there working, continuing my work, to, to make-- to perfection my--

speaker: interviewer
text: To perfect your trade.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then I came to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: A year later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A little bit later, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Where did you-- where did you settle in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I settled in Israel, Kiryat Haim-- Kiryat Haim--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you became reunited.

speaker: interviewee
text: --not far from Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you became reunited with your wife and your child at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Kiryat Haim, what kind of living quarters did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: You call this [SPEAKING HEBREW]-- academic people, people with diplomas. They make-- from Sweden, was donated little [NON-ENGLISH], little houses. It was nice, comfortable, small little houses. Comparison-- we came from Russia, we feel comfortable. And we was living in this housing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kiryat Haim.

speaker: interviewee
text: Special was-- this street was maybe two blocks, was little houses. And this is engineers and lawyers and doctors and teachers. It was over there, like a-- this, this is the place where they, they give them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work were you doing in Kiryat Haim?

speaker: interviewee
text: To tell you the truth, in the beginning, when I finished the school, I was finished-- I finished the best, top student. I was sure they will accept me like an excellent worker. When I came to Israel and they take me for a test, I couldn't pass the test or show demand and high, high qualification, which I was very disappointed. I finished with 5-plus.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a welder.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they told me I am excellent-- not good, just excellent. And in Israel, they give me a test, and I couldn't pass. Then they give me the lowest grade. Then it's OK, I think already for the lowest grade. Then I realized where I'm going to work is welding boilers, and the boilers under high pressure. And I was not prepared. I didn't learn this kind of stuff to do it, just straight weldings.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was quite specialized for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. And, and I was welding over there. I was working in a shop, [NON-ENGLISH], in, in Israel. And then they laid me off. When you're over 28, 29, they don't want to-- older people in this time to hold it. When they're holding you a year, they're obligated to hold you. They can't fire you. And-- but to fire you, to lay you off, they have to pay you lots of money. It's they-- they hold me for 11 months. And then they let me home. And then they asked me to come after a week back to work, back from the-- from the-- from the beginning. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I decided not to go to them anymore. I was disappointed. And I've gone, like the railway station, [NON-ENGLISH], I've gone over there to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the railway station in Israel--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, welding.

speaker: interviewer
text: --as a welder.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And now they did the same thing with me over there, too. After I didn't even finish the year, they let me go. They call this [NON-ENGLISH]. They don't want me to have, like, a steady job. And I was very much disappointed. I became pessimistic. I-- when you know you're capable to something else, and you have no opportunity. And my family in Israel, they're very honest people. And honest people didn't have it-- money. They make a nice, balebatish, comfortable living in Israel. Now, to help me financially, nobody was prepared.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they wish they could, as people with a good heart, they could help me. They didn't have the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were disappointed that you couldn't hold onto a position.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was disappointed with myself alone. You see, I didn't ask for nobody. I didn't have financial opportunity. I try in Israel. I-- my, my-- in my head came different kind of fantasies. And I tried to open up a new business in Israel-- coffee, coffee, to make coffee different taste, which we have it right now, called different name, different kind of coffee, and coffee to sell direct to the wholesales, groceries, not to have to do it individuals, the manufacturing. You need money to start it. And I couldn't find nobody to help me, to go in partnership. And I see it's a country, which without opportunity, and financial opportunity, you're nothing, especially when you're older. And they laid me off after 11 months. I was very disappointed. I was trying my best to, to work good. And I figured up they, they will hold me. And still they didn't hold me. And this is because the policy in this time was a very tough to get a steady job. And what happened, happened was my sister was already here. And we sent her a letter. And I told my wife, I feel we'll never go to Canada. Something opportunity will be for us. We have no chances. We have no money. We're poor people. What we make, we spend for food. I don't believe to save money for stomach. And my child's supposed to have what I-- he needed every day. And I don't drink, I don't smoke, and my wife the same thing. And we just break even with the money what we, we made. And this why we decided to leave Israel, why--

speaker: interviewer
text: To apply to--

speaker: interviewee
text: --with work is very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my wife was teaching. And was-- they considered her a very good teacher. And your money was-- teachers, at this time, received very little, similar what I received, like, a worker, same money. I didn't like to leave the country. I liked very much Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have no choice. I didn't have a choice. My sister sponsored me to Canada. And I came to Canada to prepare a little bit for my-- which I borrowed money to get a ticket. I didn't have money for a ticket.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in Canada in 1964.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you bring your wife and child with you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you bring your wife and child with you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came by myself. I-- we didn't have money. I borrowed money for ticket for myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I was working hard to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was welding. And I was working. I received minimum wage. I didn't know the language. And these people, where they could understand to speak German, or Polish, Ukrainian, or Jewish, then they used me, and I received minimum wage. For minimum wage, you can't go too far. I sent money to my wife, a little bit to pay off my debts, what I borrowed money for a ticket. And then I prepared a little bit money to make a down payment. And I was living in Polson, which $50 I paid a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your wife come over?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife came over in-- after a year I was here, in 1963. And she came over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1963, you and your wife and child were living on Polson. You--

speaker: interviewee
text: We--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you bought a home?

speaker: interviewee
text: --was-- in the beginning, we was-- one Jewish man, [? Hoffman, ?] an older man, have a bungalow. And the wife left him. He was living alone. And he told us he will not charge us too much and to live with him. I was living in Polson, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: With this--

speaker: interviewee
text: With the--

speaker: interviewer
text: --older man.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I was living in Polson. I was renting for $60 a month-- $50 a month, one bedroom. And it was not convenient. See, one washroom, and the landlady, older lady, and I feel not comfortable to use one washroom with a person what she's not looking so nice left to herself. She's older age, a nice lady. And I decided to-- I already make a little bit of money. And I saved in this time maybe $120 or $130. And I told my wife-- OK, we paid out a black-and-white TV, in monthly payments. And I told my wife, time to move out. And came opportunity, which the wife left him. And he lived in Seven Oaks, the guy. He had a house in Seven Oaks. And he said, [NON-ENGLISH], I don't need the money. Just come in, whatever you pay me, what you're paying. I charge you only $80 a month. I told my wife, $30 the difference, we're going to live comfortable. And we left. We gone to him to live-- which I lived in Seven Oaks for two years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I feel-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you see, with a older man-- my wife was washing up the floor, dirtied the floor, is coming in with his shoes not so clean. And I don't want my wife to be a housekeeper for him. And we decided to move to Perth apartments, which I received a raise. And I quit. And I start to work as salesman for [? Steiman. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Steiman? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was a salesman. And I moved to Perth apartments, in which-- the old [? Steiman ?] tried to take me without money, nothing to pay. He told me he'll take me and my wife, the family, to live with him when the wife died. And I [LAUGHS] have a bad experience to live with the old man.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told my wife no, no. We're going to cut down on food. I don't want it-- to be a housekeeper for somebody. And we was living in Perth apartments. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] can I continue?
